*Projection based on 2023 prison population. This and other figures regarding people past parole eligibility includes people in prison with sentences of more than one year who have not already been released on parole and excludes people with life sentences. Due to missing or unreported data, we are likely underestimating the percent of people past their parole eligibility year, especially for people with longer sentences. Results should be interpreted with caution.

::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} # Parole Eligibility ## How Parole Eligibility Is Determined Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person's prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Often, there is a minimum term a person must serve before becoming parole eligible. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
## In Prison Past Parole Eligibility Most recent data shows that 27 percent of people in prison were eligible for parole and incarcerated past parole eligibility at the end of the year, another 23 percent will reach parole eligibility next year, and an additional 48 percent will reach parole eligibility in more than one year. 2 percent of people had missing parole eligibility information. ```{=html}
``` ```{=html}
``` From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 22 percent. Our forecasting model projects that the percentage of people past their initial parole eligibility will increase by 1 percent. ```{=html}
```
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity ```{=html}

NO DATA
Data is not available. Louisiana submitted incomplete or missing data to the National Corrections Reporting Program in 2019.

``` ### Sex 95 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male. ```{=html}
``` ### Age 30 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old. ```{=html}
``` :::
## Offense Types 39 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 61 percent for nonviolent offenses. The most common offense types among people in prison past parole eligibility were public order offenses (21 percent) and drug offenses (21 percent). ```{=html}
```
## Sentence Lengths 45 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 and 24.9 years. ```{=html}
```
## Estimation Methodology
## Endnotes Data analysis was conducted using information from the National Corrections Reporting Program and the National Prisoner Statistics Program. See “National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP),” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, [https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-corrections-reporting-program-ncrp](https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-corrections-reporting-program-ncrp); “Prisoners - Statistical Tables,” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, [https://bjs.ojp.gov/library/publications/list?series_filter=Prisoners](https://bjs.ojp.gov/library/publications/list?series_filter=Prisoners). ::: {.cell} ::: # Prison Population ## Prison Population Trends From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 34 percent, changing from 39,445 in 2010 to 26,074 in 2022. ```{=html}
```
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity 65 percent of people in prison were Black, non-Hispanic. ```{=html}
``` ### Sex 95 percent of people in prison were male. ```{=html}
``` ### Age 31 percent of people in prison were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old. ```{=html}
``` :::
## Offense Types 53 percent of people in prison were in prison for violent offenses and 47 percent for nonviolent offenses. The most common offense type among people in prison were drug offenses (17 percent). ```{=html}
```
## Sentence Lengths 34 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 and 24.9 years. ```{=html}
``` ::: {.cell} ::: # Prison Releases ## Prison Release Trends From 2010 to 2020, the number of people released from prison decreased 21 percent, from 17,804 in 2010 to 14,128 in 2020. ```{=html}
``` ```{=html}
``` In the most recent year of data available, 60 percent of parole-eligible people released in Louisiana were released past their parole eligibility year, which is an increase of 71 percent from 2010. ```{=html}
```
## Conditional Releases Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. 90 percent of people released from prison were conditional releases. ```{=html}
```
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity ```{=html}

NO DATA
Data is not available. Louisiana submitted incomplete or missing data to the National Corrections Reporting Program in 2019.

``` ### Sex 89 percent of people released from prison were male. ```{=html}
``` ### Age 37 percent of people released from prison were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old. ```{=html}
``` :::
## Offense Types 17 percent of people released from prison were in prison for violent offenses and 83 percent for nonviolent offenses. The most common offense type among people released from prison were drug offenses (36 percent). ```{=html}
``` ::: {.cell} ::: # Disparities ## Measuring Disparities Disparities can be measured in many different ways by comparing the outcomes of different groups. This page details disparities in three types of metrics by race, ethnicity, and sex: (1\) Relative Rate Indexes (RRIs), which measure the rate at which one group experiences an outcome relative to another group,
(2\) the average number of years individuals spent behind bars past their parole eligibility date, including by offense type, and
(3\) the average time individuals served in prison, also broken down by offense.

The chart below shows the average time served in prison by offense type and sex for individuals released from prison. The largest disparity was observed among murder or nonnegligent manslaughter offenses, where men spent on average 10 years 1 month more in prison compared to women.

``` ::: ::: {.cell} :::
## Notes on Disparities **Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes** In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in incarceration past parole eligibility across race, ethnicity, and sex. The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals, and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility. **Metrics Displayed in Visualizations** Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex: - **RRI of Incarceration Past Parole Eligibility:** This metric quantifies the relative likelihood of remaining incarcerated past parole eligibility, comparing Black and Hispanic individuals to White individuals. An RRI above 1.0 signals a higher likelihood of delayed parole release, whereas an RRI below 1.0 indicates a lower likelihood. These disparities shed light on inequities in the parole release process. - **Average Time Past Parole Eligibility:** For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups. - **Average Total Time Served:** We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities. **Data Considerations** Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology. Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states: - Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations. - Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals. - Including 'Other' Category in Analysis: In states where populations categorized as "Other race(s), non-Hispanic" are substantial, we include these groups in the analysis. **Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation** Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as "Other race(s), non-Hispanic" when the sample size is too small to make meaningful comparisons. According to the NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis. ::: --- title: "Louisiana" ---
